---
title: "L01 Review"
subtitle: "Data Science 3 with R (STAT 301-3)"
author: "Hortense Ineza Mutamba"
pagetitle: "L01 Hortense Ineza Mutamba"
date: today

format:
  html:
    theme: cosmo
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 4
    toc-location: left
    embed-resources: true
    code-fold: false
    link-external-newwindow: true

execute:
  warning: false

from: markdown+emoji
reference-location: margin
citation-location: margin   
---

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## Github Repo Link

[Hortense's Github Link](https://github.com/stat301-3-2026-spring/l01-review-hortenseinezamutamba)

:::

## Overview

The goal of this lab is to review machine learning/predictive modeling vocabulary and concepts covered in Data Science 2 with R (STAT 301-2).

## Questions

When completing the following questions ensure your solutions are neatly formatted and clearly indicated. Consider including diagrams/images in some of your solutions, if it helps to make things easier to describe/discuss.

### Question 1

Provide a brief outline/overview of the steps involved in a supervised machine learning process. Could provide this as a bulleted list. 

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## Solution

* **Define the problem** : identify the prediction objective and whether it is regression or classification.
* **Collect and explore data** : inspect for missingness, class imbalance, and distribution of target variable and other potential variables.
* **Split the data** : divide into training and test sets (stratified by outcome if needed)
* **Feature engineering** : develop recipes to preprocess and transform predictors
* **Select and specify models** : choose model types relevant to use and define hyperparameters to tune.
* **Resample and tune** : use cross-validation to estimate performance and find optimal hyperparameters.
* **Compare and select** — evaluate all workflows using a primary metric and select the best model
* **Fit final model** — train winning model on full training set
* **Evaluate on test set** — assess final model performance on held-out data

:::

### Question 2

Explain the difference between supervised and unsupervised learning.

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## Solution

For **supervised learning**, the model is trained on labeled data. This means that each observation has *a known outcome variable* that the model learns to predict. Examples include regression and classification problems.

For **unsupervised learning**, there is *no outcome variable*. The model instead finds patterns, structure, or groupings within the data on its own. Examples include clustering and dimensionality reduction.

:::

### Question 3 

In general, we can classify a model by its purpose into 1 of the 3 categories below. Provide a brief description of the goals of these model classes.

#### Descriptive Models 

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## Solution

Descriptive models aim to summarize or represent patterns in data in a way that is easy to understand. The goal is not prediction or inference but rather *to describe what the data looks like*. Example: using a trend line on a scatterplot to visualize a relationship.


:::

#### Inferential Models

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## Solution

Inferential models aim to test hypotheses and understand the relationship between predictors and the outcome. The goal is *to make conclusions about the population and determine which variables have statistically significant effects*. Example: determining whether a drug treatment significantly reduces blood pressure.


:::

#### Predictive Models

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## Solution

Predictive models aim to *produce accurate predictions of the outcome variable for new, unseen observations*. The focus is on minimizing prediction error rather than interpreting relationships. Example: predicting house prices based on features like size and location.


:::

### Question 4 

We can further describe/classify predictive models by how they were derived or developed as being either mechanistic or empirically driven. 

#### Part (a)

What does it mean to be a mechanistic model?

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## Solution

YOUR SOLUTION HERE

:::

#### Part (b)

What does it mean to be an empirically driven model?

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## Solution

YOUR SOLUTION HERE

:::

#### Part (c)

How does the mechanistic and empirically driven model terminology relate to the parametric and nonparametric model terminology? 

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## Solution

YOUR SOLUTION HERE

:::

#### Part (d)

In general, is a mechanistic or empirically driven model easier to interpret? Explain.

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## Solution

YOUR SOLUTION HERE

:::

#### Part (e)

How does mechanistic and empirically driven model terminology relate to the idea of model flexibility? That is, which would be more or less flexible than the other.

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## Solution

YOUR SOLUTION HERE

:::

#### Part (f)

Describe the bias-variance trade-off when considering the use of a mechanistic or empirically driven model. 

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## Solution

YOUR SOLUTION HERE

:::

### Question 5 

Explain the difference between a regression and classification machine learning (ML) problems.

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## Solution

YOUR SOLUTION HERE

:::

### Question 6 

When splitting the data, why is it useful to stratify by the outcome/target variable? 

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## Solution

YOUR SOLUTION HERE

:::

### Question 7 

Briefly describe how v-fold cross validation with repeats is used to estimate test RMSE. Also provide an explanation of why we use it. 

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## Solution

YOUR SOLUTION HERE

:::

### Question 8

When might we use a bootstrap resampling procedure instead of v-fold cross validation to estimate test RMSE?

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## Solution

YOUR SOLUTION HERE

:::

### Question 9 

Briefly describe model tuning and why we use it.

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## Solution

YOUR SOLUTION HERE

:::

### Question 10 

What are two common performance metrics when dealing with a regression ML problem?

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## Solution

YOUR SOLUTION HERE

:::

What are two common performance metrics when dealing with a classification ML problem?

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## Solution

YOUR SOLUTION HERE

:::

### Question 11

Classify each question/problem below as either prediction or inferential. Explain your reasoning for each.

A company with a subscription based service (for example Netflix, Disney+, New York Times, etc) has data concerning customer interactions with a them, including features like the number of customer service calls, quality of service calls, subscription length, engagement with the service, discounted service, etc. They are interested in two questions:

1. Does a high number of service calls impact a customer's likelihood of churn in the next month (leave the company/drop the service)?

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## Solution

YOUR SOLUTION HERE

:::

2. How likely is it that a customer will churn in the next month (leave the company/drop the service)?

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## Solution

YOUR SOLUTION HERE

:::
